section.title: The Covenant Confirmed
section.sectionRationale: This closes the giving of the law that began at Sinai: the people ratify the covenant in blood, seventy elders see God and eat before Him, and Moses ascends into the fire and cloud to receive the stone tablets and the pattern for the tabernacle.
section.thesis: Israel binds itself to the LORD with blood and a shared meal in His presence, and Moses then enters the consuming fire on the mountain to receive the tablets of the covenant.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the people ratify the covenant with blood and a covenant meal; the elders see the God of Israel and eat in His presence; Moses ascends into the cloud and fire of Sinai for forty days.

units.label: Blood of the covenant
units.note: all that the LORD has spoken we will do

media.alt: An altar with twelve pillars beneath a mountain
media.caption: Moses builds an altar and twelve pillars, then seals the covenant with blood.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “Come up to the LORD, you and Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of Israel's elders, and you are to worship at a distance.
Moses alone shall approach the LORD, but the others must not come near. And the people may not go up with him.”
When Moses came and told the people all the words and ordinances of the LORD, they all responded with one voice: “All the words that the LORD has spoken, we will do.”
And Moses wrote down all the words of the LORD. Early the next morning he got up and built an altar at the base of the mountain, along with twelve pillars for the twelve tribes of Israel.
Then he sent out some young men of Israel, and they offered burnt offerings and sacrificed young bulls as peace offerings to the LORD.
Moses took half of the blood and put it in bowls, and the other half he sprinkled on the altar.
Then he took the {{covenant|Book of the Covenant}} and read it to the people, who replied, “All that the LORD has spoken we will do, and we will be obedient.”
So Moses took the {{blood|blood}}, sprinkled it on the people, and said, “This is the blood of the covenant that the LORD has made with you in accordance with all these words.”
Then Moses went up with Aaron, Nadab and Abihu, and seventy of the elders of Israel,
and they saw the God of Israel. Under His feet was a work like a pavement made of sapphire, as clear as the sky itself.
But God did not lay His hand on the nobles of Israel; they saw Him, and they ate and drank.

units.label: Moses ascends the mountain
units.note: the glory of the LORD, a consuming fire

media.alt: A cloud covering a mountain summit
media.caption: Moses enters the cloud where the glory of the LORD burns like a consuming fire.

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “Come up to Me on the mountain and stay here, so that I may give you the tablets of stone, with the law and commandments I have written for their instruction.”
So Moses set out with Joshua his attendant and went up on the mountain of God.
And he said to the elders, “Wait here for us until we return to you. Aaron and Hur are here with you. Whoever has a dispute can go to them.”
When Moses went up on the mountain, the cloud covered it,
and the {{glory|glory}} of the LORD settled on Mount Sinai. For six days the cloud covered it, and on the seventh day the LORD called to Moses from within the cloud.
And the sight of the glory of the LORD was like a consuming fire on the mountaintop in the eyes of the Israelites.
Moses entered the cloud as he went up on the mountain, and he remained on the mountain forty days and forty nights.

covenant.gloss: A covenant, a binding agreement sealed with obligations on both sides. The Book of the Covenant is the written record of what the LORD has just spoken, chapters 20 through 23, which the people now formally accept.

blood.gloss: Blood, the price of life offered in sacrifice. Sprinkled on both altar and people, it binds the two parties together and points to the life laid down that makes any covenant with a holy God possible.

glory.gloss: Glory, weightiness, the visible splendor of God's presence. Here it settles on Sinai as a consuming fire, showing that the God who now dwells among His people remains utterly holy and dangerous to approach carelessly.

basic.context: This chapter closes the giving of the law at Sinai. The people formally agree to the covenant, Moses seals it with blood, and then seventy elders go up the mountain and actually see God and share a meal in His presence. After that, Moses goes further up alone into the fire and cloud to receive the stone tablets and instructions for the tabernacle.
basic.hermeneutics: This really happened at a real mountain to a real, newly redeemed nation. It is the ratification ceremony for the old covenant, a specific historical event, though the pattern it sets, that God binds Himself to His people through blood and shares a meal with them, echoes forward into the New Testament.
basic.originalLanguages: The berith, covenant, is sealed with dam, blood, sprinkled on both the altar and the people, showing that both God and Israel are bound by this agreement through a costly sacrifice. Later, on the mountain, the kavod, glory, of the LORD appears as fire, showing His presence is both real and dangerous to those unprepared.
basic.typology: The blood of the covenant sprinkled on the people points forward to the blood of Christ, which Jesus Himself names at the Last Supper. The elders eating and drinking in God's presence anticipates the fuller fellowship believers now have with God and the future marriage supper of the Lamb.
basic.christ: At the Last Supper, Jesus takes the cup and says, 'This is My blood of the covenant,' deliberately echoing Moses' words here, Matthew 26:28. What Moses sealed with animal blood at Sinai, Christ seals forever with His own blood, establishing the new covenant that this old one anticipated.
basic.god: God is both near and terrifying: He allows elders to see Him and eat in His presence, yet His glory burns on the mountain like consuming fire, and only Moses may approach further. He binds Himself in covenant to His people while remaining utterly holy.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 26:27-28 records Jesus using the same covenant language over the cup. Hebrews 9:18-22 explains that the first covenant was not put into effect without blood. Hebrews 12:18-24 contrasts the terror of Sinai with the access believers now have to God through Christ.
basic.oneStory: God seals His covenant with Israel in blood and lets its leaders eat in His presence, a real but partial fellowship. That pattern of covenant blood and a shared meal finds its fulfillment in Jesus, whose blood establishes the new covenant and whose table welcomes His people fully into God's presence.

context.literary: This chapter forms the hinge between the law given in chapters 20 through 23 and the tabernacle instructions that begin in chapter 25. It moves from covenant ratification at the base of the mountain, to a startling vision of God by the elders, to Moses' extended ascent into the cloud.
context.historical: This is the formal ratification of the Sinai covenant, the ancient near eastern equivalent of both parties signing a binding treaty, sealed here not with ink but with sacrificial blood and a shared meal, the customary way ancient covenants between parties were confirmed.
context.canonical: Exodus 24:8, 'the blood of the covenant,' is quoted directly by Jesus at the Last Supper and referenced throughout Hebrews as it explains how the old covenant, ratified in blood, anticipates the new covenant ratified in Christ's blood.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the historical ratification of the Sinai covenant and to show that a holy God can genuinely draw near to His covenant people through blood and mediation, while still remaining set apart in consuming glory.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a one time historical ratification ceremony, not a repeatable pattern Christians are commanded to reenact. Its abiding truth is that covenant with God is costly, requires blood, and yet results in real fellowship, a truth fulfilled once for all in Christ.

debates.question: What does it mean that the elders 'saw the God of Israel' and yet lived, when elsewhere Scripture says no one can see God's face and live?

views.label: A veiled or partial theophany
views.case: They saw a visible manifestation of God's presence, likely a form beneath which His unapproachable essence was hidden, consistent with Exodus 33:20 that no one sees God's face directly and lives.

views.label: An appearance of the pre-incarnate Christ
views.case: Some see this as a Christophany, an appearance of the Son before His incarnation, since the New Testament teaches that no one has seen the Father, John 1:18, yet the Son has been seen at various points in the Old Testament.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text affirms a real, gracious encounter with God that stops short of full unmediated vision, consistent with the rest of Scripture's teaching on God's transcendence and nearness.

study.originalLanguages: The berith is sealed in two stages: first the people verbally commit, then Moses confirms it with dam, sprinkled blood, the price of life given so that sinful people might be bound to a holy God. Later the kavod, weighty glory, of the LORD appears on the mountain as fire, a visible but unapproachable holiness.

typology.type: The blood of the covenant, Exodus 24:8
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in Christ's blood of the new covenant, poured out for many, Matthew 26:28, Hebrews 9:15.

typology.type: The elders eating and drinking in God's presence
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates full fellowship with God through Christ and the marriage supper of the Lamb, Revelation 19:9.

typology.type: Moses entering the consuming fire and cloud alone
typology.fulfillment: Points to Christ, the one true Mediator who alone enters fully into God's presence on behalf of His people, Hebrews 9:24.

study.christ: Jesus consciously takes up the words of this chapter at the Last Supper: 'This is My blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins,' Matthew 26:28. Where the blood of bulls sprinkled at Sinai could not ultimately cleanse the conscience, Hebrews 9:13-14 explains that the blood of Christ does, establishing the covenant this scene only foreshadowed.
study.god: God binds Himself to Israel in a real covenant, allowing elders to behold Him and share a meal in His presence, a remarkable act of condescension. Yet His glory on the mountain burns like consuming fire, showing that nearness to God never means He has become any less holy.

crossReferences.note: Jesus takes the cup and names it the blood of the covenant, deliberately echoing Exodus 24:8.
Explains that even the first covenant was inaugurated with blood, and without the shedding of blood there is no forgiveness.
Contrasts the fire and terror of Sinai with the access to God's presence believers now have through Jesus, the mediator of a new covenant.

study.oneStory: At Sinai, God seals His covenant with Israel through sacrificial blood and lets its elders taste real fellowship with Him, even as His glory burns on the mountain in holy fire. This pattern, costly blood opening the way to God's presence, reaches its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, whose blood establishes the new covenant and opens permanent access to the Father.

quiz.q: How did the people respond when Moses told them all the words and ordinances of the LORD?

options.text: With one voice, they said, 'All the words that the LORD has spoken, we will do.'
options.note: Verse 3 records this unanimous response before Moses even writes the words down.

options.text: They asked for more time to consider the covenant terms.
options.note: No delay is recorded; their commitment was immediate and unanimous.

options.text: Only the elders agreed, while the rest of the people were silent.
options.note: The text says all the people responded with one voice, not only the elders.

options.text: They refused and asked Moses to renegotiate the terms.
options.note: The opposite occurred; they willingly agreed to obey all that the LORD had spoken.

quiz.q: How did Moses seal the covenant between the LORD and the people?

options.text: By sprinkling half the sacrificial blood on the altar and half on the people.
options.note: Verses 6 and 8 describe this two part sprinkling, calling it the blood of the covenant.

options.text: By having each tribal leader sign a written document.
options.note: Moses did write down the words, but the covenant was sealed with blood, not signatures.

options.text: By offering only incense on the altar.
options.note: Burnt offerings and peace offerings were made, and it was the blood, not incense, that sealed the covenant.

options.text: No sealing act took place.
options.note: Verses 6 through 8 explicitly describe the blood ceremony that sealed the covenant.

quiz.q: What happened when Moses, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and the seventy elders went up the mountain?

options.text: They saw the God of Israel and ate and drank in His presence without harm.
options.note: Verses 10 to 11 record this remarkable, gracious encounter.

options.text: They were struck down for approaching too closely.
options.note: The text states plainly that God did not lay His hand on them; they were not harmed.

options.text: They saw nothing but heard only thunder.
options.note: The text specifically says they saw the God of Israel, more than mere thunder.

options.text: They were forbidden from ascending at all.
options.note: Verse 9 explicitly says Moses went up with Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and the seventy elders.

quiz.q: How long did Moses remain on the mountain after entering the cloud?

options.text: Forty days and forty nights.
options.note: Verse 18 states this exact duration.

options.text: Seven days only.
options.note: The cloud covered the mountain for six days before the LORD called to Moses on the seventh, but Moses himself remained forty days and nights.

options.text: A single day.
options.note: The stay was far longer, forty days and nights.

options.text: The text does not specify a duration.
options.note: Verse 18 explicitly gives the forty day, forty night duration.

quiz.q: What did the glory of the LORD look like to the Israelites on the mountaintop?

options.text: Like a consuming fire.
options.note: Verse 17 describes it exactly this way, in the sight of all Israel.

options.text: Like a gentle mist with no visible color.
options.note: The text specifically describes fire, something far more overwhelming than a gentle mist.

options.text: Completely invisible to everyone but Moses.
options.note: The text says the Israelites themselves saw the sight of the glory of the LORD like consuming fire.

options.text: No visible sign accompanied God's presence.
options.note: Verse 17 explicitly describes the visible sight of consuming fire.

questions: The people said, 'All the words that the LORD has spoken, we will do,' before they had faced any real test of that promise. Where in your own life have you made commitments to God more easily than you have kept them?
The covenant was sealed with blood, not merely words. How does remembering that your relationship with God cost the blood of Christ change the way you approach Him today?

The elders saw God and ate in His presence without being struck down, a real but bounded nearness. Where do you find yourself either presuming too much on God's nearness or fearing to draw near at all?
Moses left ordinary life behind for forty days to be with God on the mountain. What would it look like for you to give God unhurried, extended time this week rather than only brief, hurried moments?
God's glory appeared like consuming fire, both real and dangerous. How does holding together God's nearness and His holiness shape the way you pray and worship?

translationNotes.note: The Book of the Covenant most likely refers to the laws just given in chapters 20 through 23, which Moses had written down in verse 4; it is read aloud so the people know exactly what they are agreeing to.

Under His feet was a work like a pavement of sapphire describes what the elders saw beneath God, not His face or full form; the language is deliberately restrained, consistent with later statements that no one sees God's face and lives.

God did not lay His hand on the nobles of Israel is a Hebrew idiom meaning He did not strike them down, emphasizing that this vision of God, unlike other encounters with His holiness, brought no harm.
